From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Bog consisting of ridges and islands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Kepler_Mire_-_Dead_Marshes.JPG/250px-Kepler_Mire_-_Dead_Marshes.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kepler_Mire_-_Dead_Marshes.JPG)

The [Kepler Mire](/wiki/Kepler_Mire "Kepler Mire"), a string bog in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").

A **string bog** or **string mire** is a [bog](/wiki/Bog "Bog") consisting of slightly elevated ridges and islands, with woody plants, alternating with flat, wet [sedge](/wiki/Sedge "Sedge") mat areas. String bogs occur on slightly sloping surfaces, with the ridges at right angles to the direction of water flow. They are an example of [patterned vegetation](/wiki/Patterned_vegetation "Patterned vegetation").

String bogs are also known as **aapa moors** or **aapa mires** (from [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") *aapasuo*) or **Strangmoor** (from the [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

A string bog has a pattern of narrow (2–3m wide), low (less than 1m high) ridges oriented at right angles to the direction of drainage with wet depressions or pools occurring between the ridges. The water and peat are very low in nutrients because the water has been derived from other [ombrotrophic](/wiki/Ombrotrophic "Ombrotrophic") wetlands, which receive all of their water and nutrients from precipitation rather than from streams or springs.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The peat thickness is greater than 1m.

String bogs are features associated with [periglacial](/wiki/Periglacial "Periglacial") climates, where the temperature results in long periods of subzero temperatures. The active layer exists as a frozen ground for long periods and melts in the spring thaw. Slow melting produces characteristic mass movement processes and features associated with specific periglacial environments.
